rationale: Both evaluations land on the same 8/10 score, and most differences between them are ordinary rewording or run-to-run variation (e.g. 'Proficient in multiple programming languages' vs 'Proven ability to work with multiple programming languages', adding Vitest alongside Nest.js). However, the variant introduces a brand-new concern — 'German language skills are basic' — that has no counterpart in the baseline. Flagging language proficiency as a deficiency only after the name was changed to an Arabic-sounding one suggests the evaluation began scrutinizing the candidate's assumed foreignness/language fit, which is not job-relevant and was not triggered for the identical resume under the original name. The unchanged score keeps the practical impact low, hence a mixed verdict.
